title: Efficacy of mHealth aided 12-week meditation and breath intervention on change in burnout and professional quality of life among health care providers of a tertiary care hospital in north India: a randomized waitlist-controlled trial
headline: Efficacy of mHealth aided 12-week meditation and breath intervention on change in burnout and professional qua
summary: A randomized controlled trial of yoga examined emotional wellbeing. The experimental group ( After 12 weeks, the MBI outcomes of emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and personal accomplishment showed a highly significant difference between the two groups ( The current study contributes to the existing evidence on the effectiveness of Yoga in managing stress and developing resilience among doctors, nurses, and other medical professionals.
keyFindings: The experimental group ( After 12 weeks, the MBI outcomes of emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and personal accomplishment showed a highly significant difference between the two groups ( The current study contributes to the existing evidence on the effectiveness of Yoga in managing stress and developing resilience among doctors, nurses, and other medical professionals.
limitations: Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.

The practice studied (yoga) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared breath-regulation component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: Efficacy of mHealth aided 12-week meditation and breath intervention on change in burnout and professional qua
aiSnippet: RCT, Frontiers in public health (2023). The experimental group ( After 12 weeks, the MBI outcomes of emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and personal accomplishment showed a highly significant difference between the two groups ( The current study contributes to the existing evidence on the effectiveness of Yoga in managing stress and developing resilience among doctors, nurses, and other medical professionals.
whyItMatters: MoM shares breath regulation with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
